module_title: Hosting web APIs with Azure App Service

choices.prompt: In Azure App Service, what is the App Service Plan according to the module?
choices: The set of compute resources (a tier and a number of instances) that one or more web apps share, defining horsepower and feature set
The deployed application workload itself, including your code or container image
A staging copy of your app with its own hostname used for zero-downtime releases
A managed certificate store that terminates TLS on behalf of your app
correct_answers: The set of compute resources (a tier and a number of instances) that one or more web apps share, defining horsepower and feature set

choices.prompt: A team on a Basic-tier plan finds the "Add slot" button is missing. Based on the module, what is the correct explanation and fix?
choices: Deployment slots are a Standard-and-above feature; scale the plan up to at least Standard (S1) to enable slots
Slots require the Free tier to be disabled first; downgrade billing then re-add the slot
The app name is not globally unique; rename the app and the slot option will reappear
HTTPS-only must be turned off before slots can be created; set --https-only false first
correct_answers: Deployment slots are a Standard-and-above feature; scale the plan up to at least Standard (S1) to enable slots

choices.prompt: After a slot swap, production suddenly connects to the staging database. According to the module, what configuration mistake most likely caused this?
choices: The database connection setting was not marked as a deployment-slot (sticky) setting, so it followed the code from staging into production during the swap
The staging slot was not warmed up before the swap, so production served stale connections
The app settings were baked into the deployment image, overriding the platform values
HTTPS-only was enabled, which redirected the production connection string to staging
correct_answers: The database connection setting was not marked as a deployment-slot (sticky) setting, so it followed the code from staging into production during the swap

choices.prompt: Which statements about App Service application settings and secrets are accurate per the module? (Select all that apply.)
choices: App Service injects application settings into your process as environment variables at startup
Settings configured in the platform override matching values baked into your deployment
App settings stored as plaintext are fully secure and need no further protection, since they are out of source control
For real secrets, the module recommends referencing Key Vault rather than storing raw values
correct_answers: App Service injects application settings into your process as environment variables at startup
Settings configured in the platform override matching values baked into your deployment
For real secrets, the module recommends referencing Key Vault rather than storing raw values

choices.prompt: In the Northwind Parcel walkthrough, which steps and details are correct as shown in the module? (Select all that apply.)
choices: A Standard plan (S1) is created because the Standard tier is required for slots
The --https-only true setting causes any http:// request to be redirected
The swap command exchanges the staging slot with the production target slot for a zero-downtime release
The web app's globally unique name requirement can be ignored because *.azurewebsites.net hostnames are scoped per resource group
correct_answers: A Standard plan (S1) is created because the Standard tier is required for slots
The --https-only true setting causes any http:// request to be redirected
The swap command exchanges the staging slot with the production target slot for a zero-downtime release

choices.prompt: According to the module, what kind of hosting model is Azure App Service and what does it handle on your behalf?
choices: A Platform-as-a-Service host that runs your code or container on infrastructure it patches, load-balances, and keeps available
An Infrastructure-as-a-Service offering where you manage the operating-system updates and virtual machines yourself
A Function-as-a-Service runtime that executes code only when an event occurs
A managed Kubernetes cluster where you define pods and handle node patching directly
correct_answers: A Platform-as-a-Service host that runs your code or container on infrastructure it patches, load-balances, and keeps available

choices.prompt: The module warns about 'swapping without warming up.' What problem does it say this causes, and what does it recommend doing first?
choices: An un-warmed slot can serve slow first requests right after a swap; hit the staging slot's health endpoint before swapping so instances are ready
The swap will fail outright because App Service blocks swaps on cold instances until a warm-up timeout passes
Production will return HTTP 500 errors during the swap; disable HTTPS-only first so traffic can reach the cold slot
The staging database connection becomes sticky automatically; mark it non-sticky before swapping to avoid slow requests
correct_answers: An un-warmed slot can serve slow first requests right after a swap; hit the staging slot's health endpoint before swapping so instances are ready

choices.prompt: Per the module, why is reusing a generic app name like 'orders-api' a pitfall, and what is the recommended fix?
choices: The default *.azurewebsites.net hostname is global, so a generic name will already be taken; use an org prefix
Generic names are reserved for the Free tier only, so you must upgrade to Standard before using one
App Service blocks names shorter than ten characters; lengthen the name to satisfy the validator
Non-unique names disable deployment slots; rename the app to restore the 'Add slot' option
correct_answers: The default *.azurewebsites.net hostname is global, so a generic name will already be taken; use an org prefix

choices.prompt: Which statements about a deployment slot and the swap are accurate according to the module? (Select all that apply.)
choices: A deployment slot is a fully functioning copy of your app with its own hostname, running inside the same plan
After a swap, the old version sits in staging, so a swap back is instant if the new version misbehaves
App Service warms up the staging instances and then redirects production traffic to them by exchanging the routing
Each deployment slot requires its own separate App Service Plan with dedicated compute instances
correct_answers: A deployment slot is a fully functioning copy of your app with its own hostname, running inside the same plan
After a swap, the old version sits in staging, so a swap back is instant if the new version misbehaves
App Service warms up the staging instances and then redirects production traffic to them by exchanging the routing

choices.prompt: Which statements about configuration in App Service, as presented in the module, are correct? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Application settings let the same image behave differently per environment because platform settings override values baked into the deployment
Connection strings are a closely related slot in the configuration UI that App Service can surface with a documented prefix
The module frames configuration and secrets as operational data rather than source code
For most modern apps the module recommends connection strings over plain app settings plus a secret store as the cleaner path
correct_answers: Application settings let the same image behave differently per environment because platform settings override values baked into the deployment
Connection strings are a closely related slot in the configuration UI that App Service can surface with a documented prefix
The module frames configuration and secrets as operational data rather than source code

llm.prompt: Explain how a deployment slot swap achieves a zero-downtime release in Azure App Service, and walk through what happens to the production and staging slots before, during, and after the swap.
llm.reference_answer: A deployment slot is a fully functioning copy of the app with its own hostname, running inside the same App Service Plan. You deploy the new version to the staging slot, let it warm up, and smoke-test it against its own URL (e.g. https://northwind-orders-api-staging.azurewebsites.net). When you run the swap, App Service first warms up the staging instances, then redirects production traffic to them by exchanging the routing, so users never hit a cold or half-deployed app. After the swap the production hostname serves the new build while the old version now sits in staging, which means a swap back is instant if the new version misbehaves. Because App Service warmed the instances before redirecting traffic, production returns no errors during the release.

llm.prompt: Compare 'sticky' (deployment-slot) settings with normal settings during a swap, and explain why getting this distinction wrong can point production at the wrong database.
llm.reference_answer: Normal (non-sticky) settings move with the code during a swap, so a value configured in staging follows the deployment into production when slots are exchanged. Settings marked as deployment-slot settings (called 'sticky') instead stay pinned to their slot during a swap, which is useful for keeping a staging connection string out of production. If a database connection string is left non-sticky when it should be slot-specific, the swap carries the staging value into production, so production ends up connecting to the staging database. The fix is to deliberately mark environment-specific values, such as connection strings, as deployment-slot settings so they remain pinned to their slot and only the intended code moves during the swap.

llm.prompt: Walk through why choosing an App Service tier is a feature decision and not just a price decision, and explain the related pitfall of treating the tier as a price knob only.
llm.reference_answer: App Service tiers range from Free and Basic up through Standard, Premium, and Isolated, and the practical rule is that features like deployment slots and autoscaling appear at Standard and above. So when you choose a tier you are choosing a feature set and a scaling ceiling, not just a price. The pitfall is treating the tier as a price knob only: picking Basic to save money silently removes slots and autoscaling, which can leave a team without zero-downtime releases or scale-out capability and confused when the 'Add slot' button is missing. The correct approach is to choose the tier by the features the workload actually needs, then optimize cost within that tier, for example using a Standard (S1) plan when slots are required. Exact per-tier instance sizes and limits should be confirmed in the current docs because Microsoft revises them.
